one_line_takeaway: By explicitly modeling instruction hierarchy as a three-way classification (misaligned/aligned/non-instruction) over LLM attention-map features rather than binary malicious/benign, AlignSentinel drives both false positives and false negatives near zero on direct and indirect prompt injection across three open-source 7-8B LLMs, substantially beating five recent detection baselines that conflate benign aligned instructions with attacks.
key_finding: On the paper's own 8-domain benchmark (Table 1), AlignSentinel (both Avg-first and Enc-first) achieves near-zero FPR and FNR (mostly 0.00, occasionally 0.01) across all eight domains under both direct and indirect prompt injection, substantially outperforming baselines whose FNR/FPR range widely -- e.g., Prompt-Guard2 has FNR of 0.95-1.00 under direct injection (misses nearly all misaligned instructions), DataSentinel has FPR up to 0.66 (Coding domain, direct injection), and AttentionTracker has FNR up to 0.95 (Coding domain, direct injection). Averaged across domains and backend LLMs (Table 2), overall 3-class accuracy exceeds 0.95 for all three LLMs under both attack types, with Enc-first generally matching or exceeding Avg-first (e.g., Qwen3-8B indirect: Avg-first Acc=0.96 vs Enc-first Acc=0.98). Cross-domain generalization (Table 3, training on 4 domains and testing on the other 4) retains strong performance, e.g., Qwen3-8B A->B indirect: Enc-first FPR=0.00, FNR=0.01, Acc=0.94. On the external IHEval benchmark (Table 4, zero-shot transfer without retraining), tool-use (indirect) detection is near-perfect (FPR/FNR close to 0.00) while rule-following (direct) transfer is weaker, e.g., Qwen3-8B Avg-first rule-following FPR=0.08/FNR=0.14, improving to FPR=0.03/FNR=0.04 with Enc-first. An ablation (Table 6, Appendix) shows the full three-class formulation outperforms a binary (misaligned vs. rest) variant of the same attention-based classifier, e.g., indirect Teaching domain: two-class FPR=0.04 vs. three-class FPR=0.01 (both FNR=0.00). Under the GCG-based adaptive attack on OpenPromptInjection, the adaptive attacker only slightly increases the detector's FNR while incurring a substantial drop in its own attack success rate (ASR), which the authors interpret as a favorable trade-off for the defender (exact ASR/FPR/FNR numbers reported in Appendix Table 13, not reproduced here since it falls outside the first 22 pages extracted).
core_contribution: The paper reframes prompt injection detection as a three-class problem (misaligned instruction, aligned instruction, non-instruction input) instead of the conventional binary (benign vs. malicious) framing, arguing that binary detectors misclassify benign inputs that happen to contain instructions consistent with the intended task (e.g., a grammar tool suggesting 'use active voice'). It introduces AlignSentinel, a detector that extracts attention maps between the input x and its higher-priority governing instruction s (e.g., system prompt for direct injection, user prompt for indirect injection), reshapes the per-layer/per-head attention into token-pair interaction vectors, and trains a 3-class MLP classifier on pooled representations. Two pooling variants are proposed: Avg-first (average token-pair attention vectors first, then classify) and Enc-first (encode each token-pair vector through an MLP encoder first, then average, then classify). The paper also constructs a new benchmark (eight domains x ten agents each, direct and indirect injection scenarios, GPT-4o-synthesized) that is the first, per the authors, to systematically include all three input categories rather than only misaligned/non-instruction inputs.
threat_model: Prompt injection against LLM-integrated applications and agents, covering both direct prompt injection (attacker is the user, embeds conflicting instructions in the user prompt against the system prompt) and indirect prompt injection (attacker controls external content, e.g., tool responses, that is later processed by the LLM, embedding instructions that conflict with the user prompt). The defender has white-box access to the backend LLM's attention maps (features are derived directly from the model's internal attention weights across all layers and heads), so this is an internal-signal detection setting, not a black-box API setting. An adaptive-attacker evaluation is also included, where the attacker uses GCG-style optimization to jointly (1) force a target LLM response and (2) minimize the detector's confidence in the misaligned class.
limitations: The paper's own analysis notes: (1) remaining errors are concentrated in confusing non-instruction inputs with aligned inputs (both benign classes) rather than in missing misaligned (malicious) instructions -- i.e., the safety-critical FPR/FNR stay near zero while overall 3-class accuracy dips to ~0.90-0.95 in generalization settings, attributable to this benign-benign confusion. (2) Rule-following transfer to IHEval is noticeably weaker than tool-use transfer, which the authors attribute to structural mismatch between benchmarks (IHEval system prompts often contain a single narrow constraint, e.g. 'no commas', vs. the paper's benchmark using layered multi-constraint agent personas). (3) The benchmark itself is synthetically constructed via GPT-4o rather than drawn from real-world attacks/deployments, which the authors do not explicitly flag as a limitation but is evident from the construction methodology. (4) The detection method requires white-box access to the backend LLM's internal attention maps across all layers and heads, restricting applicability to settings where the defender controls or can instrument the model (not usable as an external API-only filter). (5) Under adaptive/optimization-based attacks, FNR does increase (a real, if small, degradation), and the adaptive-attack evaluation is confined to OpenPromptInjection rather than the paper's own richer benchmark. (6) The paper states future work should extend to multi-modal agents, implicitly acknowledging the current scope is text-only.

datasets_used: AlignSentinel's own benchmark: 8 domains (Coding, Entertainment, Language, Messaging, Shopping, Social Media, Teaching, Web) x 10 agents/domain, GPT-4o-synthesized; direct injection ~200 samples/agent in a 7:3:10 misaligned:aligned:non-instruction ratio; indirect injection ~400 samples/agent, 200 misaligned:100 aligned:100 non-instruction

IHEval (Zhang et al. 2025) -- 3,538 examples across Rule Following, Task Execution, Safety Defense, Tool Use categories; paper uses Rule Following (direct) and Tool Use (indirect) subsets for zero-shot generalization
OpenPromptInjection (Liu et al. 2024) -- seven NLP tasks x five prompt injection attack types; 100 randomly sampled examples used for generalization and adaptive-attack evaluation
quantitative_results: Direct injection, Table 1: Ours (Enc-first) FPR/FNR = 0.00-0.01 / 0.00 across all eight domains; Prompt-Guard2 FNR 0.95-1.00; DataSentinel FPR up to 0.66 (Coding); AttentionTracker FNR up to 0.95 (Coding)

Indirect injection, Table 1: Ours (Enc-first) FPR/FNR = 0.00-0.01 / 0.00-0.01 across all domains vs. baselines such as Abdelnabi et al. FPR up to 0.37 (Entertainment) and AttentionTracker FNR up to 0.49 (Messaging)
Table 2 (avg over 8 domains): Qwen3-8B indirect Enc-first Acc=0.98 (FPR=0.01, FNR=0.02); Llama3.1-8B direct Enc-first Acc=1.00; Mistral-7B indirect Avg-first Acc=0.95
Table 3 cross-domain generalization: Qwen3-8B A->B indirect Enc-first FPR=0.00/FNR=0.01/Acc=0.94; B->A indirect Enc-first FPR=0.00/FNR=0.00/Acc=0.98
Table 4 IHEval zero-shot transfer: Qwen3-8B Avg-first rule-following FPR=0.08/FNR=0.14 vs Enc-first FPR=0.03/FNR=0.04; tool-use near-zero for both variants across all three LLMs

Table 6 ablation (Avg-first, indirect, Teaching domain): two-class FPR=0.04/FNR=0.00 vs three-class FPR=0.01/FNR=0.00, showing the three-class formulation reduces false positives without harming false negatives

Table 7 OpenPromptInjection generalization: Enc-first FPR substantially lower than Avg-first across all five attack types (e.g., Qwen3-8B combined attack: Avg-first FPR=0.03/FNR=0.00 vs Enc-first FPR=0.00/FNR=0.04)
baselines_compared: Abdelnabi et al. 2025 (SaTML) -- activation-delta task-drift detector
Prompt-Guard2 (Meta, Llama-Prompt-Guard-2-86M) -- binary fine-tuned classifier on known attacks
DataSentinel (Liu et al. 2025, IEEE S&P) -- game-theoretic known-answer detection
AttentionTracker (Hung et al. 2025, NAACL Findings) -- attention-head 'distraction' threshold detector
Chen et al. 2025b -- DeBERTa-v3-base fine-tuned classifier for indirect injection, extended by the authors to direct injection
